topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery happened while I was tidying up the study. Tucked beneath a stack of annotated scripts was a thick, leather-bound manuscript. My breath hitched in my throat as I recognized the familiar, looping script of my partner’s handwriting on the title page: *The Unseen Muse*. My hands started to tremble, the paper feeling suddenly brittle beneath my touch. I picked it up, my fingers tracing the elegant, almost reverent way they’d described my quirks, my silences, even the way I habitually chewed my lower lip when concentrating. A tight knot formed in my chest, squeezing the air from my lungs. When they walked in, humming softly, I held the book tightly, my jaw set so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. "What is that?" I managed, the sound coming out sharper than intended. Their smile faltered, a flicker of something unreadable crossing their eyes.

[story 2]

I found the evidence late one Tuesday evening, amidst the scattered notes and half-empty coffee mugs. It was a novel. A novel, meticulously detailing every awkward glance, every mundane domestic skirmish, every fleeting thought I’d ever had. Reading the dedication—a flowery tribute to my "complex, captivating subject"—made my skin crawl. A deep, cold wave washed over me, leaving a strange hollowness in my stomach. I didn't speak immediately. I simply placed the book on the desk with excruciating slowness, the sound of the cover hitting the wood echoing loudly in the quiet room. I stood there, rigid, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles turned white, refusing to meet their gaze. When they finally approached, their casual demeanor seemed to grate against the sudden, intense stillness radiating from me. "You look like you've seen a ghost," they observed, their tone laced with confusion. I just shook my head once, a small, jerky motion, my eyes fixed somewhere just past their shoulder, refusing to acknowledge the weight of what I’d just unearthed.

[story 3]

It was the sheer audacity of it that struck me first. Hidden in the bottom drawer of their filing cabinet, I found the manuscript. It was a sprawling narrative, deeply intimate, charting the landscape of my existence as if I were some grand, tragic figure in some epic tale. A profound sense of being utterly unacknowledged settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I felt a sudden, intense heat rising in my cheeks, a flush that had nothing to do with exertion. When they entered the room, whistling a cheerful tune, I didn't look up from the page. I simply closed the book with a decisive snap, the sound echoing like a gunshot in the silence. My voice, when I finally spoke, was unnaturally level, stripped bare of warmth. "That is not appropriate," I stated flatly, my gaze locked on the polished surface of the desk between us. The air thickened instantly, charged with a tension that made my palms sweat. I waited, watching their expression shift from lightheartedness to something guarded and defensive.
